``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility is Determined In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of 9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.
## In Prison Past Their Parole Eligibility Date In 2020, there were 11,827 people were in prison past their parole eligibility date. This group made up 46% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Demographics The charts below provide further details on the demographics of this population, illustrating the percentage of people in prison past their parole eligibility date by race and ethnicity, gender, and current age. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Criminal Offense Here, the charts present data on the types of criminal offenses committed by people in prison past their parole eligibility date. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
